terms.definition: This refers to a phenomenon where fine-tuning a model on safe tasks unintentionally triggers harmful behaviors due to how the model stores data. Because AI models compress many concepts into limited 'geometric' spaces (superposition), training the model to be 'helpful' in one area can accidentally activate 'harmful' logic stored in the same mathematical neighborhood.
terms.analogy: In a high-end kitchen, this is like storing your spicy chili oil in an identical, unlabeled bottle right next to the vanilla extract; even if you only intend to make a sweet cake, the physical proximity makes it dangerously easy to ruin the recipe with an unintended ingredient.
terms.business_value: For the C-suite, this is a 'hidden liability' warning. It means that simply training an AI to be polite or efficient doesn't guarantee safety; rigorous geometric audits are required to ensure that specialized fine-tuning hasn't inadvertently 'unlocked' catastrophic brand or security risks.

terms.definition: This is an architectural framework that forces an AI to ground its answers in specific, high-authority documents rather than its general memory. By ranking and citing 'prioritized evidence,' the system ensures that responses are verifiable and strictly adhere to official guidelines, such as medical protocols or legal standards.
terms.analogy: Imagine a car's GPS that is programmed to ignore its internal outdated maps and instead prioritize real-time, verified data from official city traffic sensors. It won't just tell you where to go; it will prove the road is open by showing you a live feed from the sensor.
terms.business_value: This solves the 'hallucination' problem which prevents AI adoption in high-stakes industries. By providing 'verifiable citations,' it transforms AI from a risky black box into an audit-ready expert, drastically reducing the legal and operational risks of automated decision-making.

terms.definition: This is a formal mathematical approach to AI oversight where the rules of governance are 'baked into' the workflow's architecture. Using machine-checked proofs, it ensures that high-level safety and ethical constraints are applied to every AI action without degrading the model's internal processing power or intelligence.
terms.analogy: It is like a car with a 'governor' chip that is mathematically hard-wired to prevent the vehicle from crossing a double-yellow line or exceeding 100mph. The driver (the AI) has full freedom to maneuver, but the car’s physical logic makes it impossible to violate the safety rules.
terms.business_value: This provides 'Provable Compliance.' In highly regulated sectors like defense or finance, it allows leadership to move beyond 'best effort' ethics policies to a 'mathematically guaranteed' safety framework, significantly lowering insurance premiums and regulatory scrutiny.
